coredata.dc:title: Potential of chitosan for targeted mitochondrial delivery of therapeutic agents

Malviya, Rishabha
Rizg, Waleed Y.
Warsi, Musarrat Husain
coredata.dc:description:
Background Chitosan, an innately sourced polymer, is esteemed for its biological compatibility and breakdown. Its cationic properties enable good interaction with diverse biological molecules, particularly beneficial in drug-delivery systems. Its capacity to target intracellular components, such as mitochondria, renders it a crucial substance in biomedical research.
Aim This paper seeks to examine the efficacy of chitosan-based nanoparticles for targeted medication delivery to mitochondria, particularly in the context of cancer treatment. The aim is to improve drug bioavailability, stability, and efficacy through the chemical modification of chitosan. The document additionally explores its extensive applications in gene therapy and vaccination administration.
Discussion The electrostatic characteristics of chitosan and its capacity to create diverse structures such as hydrogels and nanoparticles facilitate targeted medication release and enhanced cellular absorption. This biopolymer improves the specificity of cancer cells while reducing toxicity to healthy cells. The paper examines chitosan's interaction with cellular membranes and mitochondria to optimize therapeutic results.
Conclusion Chitosan nanoparticles have considerable promise in the targeted delivery of drugs to mitochondria, especially in the context of cancer therapy. Their cationic properties enhance bioavailability; yet, issues related to solubility and safety require resolution. Further study is required to optimize these nanoparticles for safe and effective application in clinical environments.

row.entry: Biocompatibility, biodegradability, mucoadhesive properties for enhanced cellular uptake.
Limited mechanical strength, low solubility in neutral and alkaline pH environments (Kim et al., 2018)
High surface area for drug loading, controllable pore sizes for sustained release.
Potential cytotoxicity due to non-degradability, complex preparation methods required (Kankala et al., 2020, Jun)
High biocompatibility, excellent for bone integration due to similarity to bone minerals.
Brittleness limits load-bearing applications, difficult to achieve homogeneous structure (Nayak et al., 2019 Jan, 1)
Biodegradable, approved for use in biomedical applications, good mechanical properties.
Low bioactivity, poor cell adhesion, and limited by slow degradation in vivo (Pang et al., 2010, Nov).
Effective in photothermal therapies, good stability, and ease of surface modification.
Expensive, potential toxicity if not properly surface-modified or size-controlled (Pham et al., 2002 Jun, 11)
Excellent biocompatibility, affordable, tunable optical properties for imaging.
Limited stability in aqueous environments, low control over size and surface properties (Lim et al., 2015)

Overcoming drug resistance in lung cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chitosan, an innately sourced polymer, is esteemed for its biological compatibility and breakdown. Its cationic properties enable good interaction with diverse biological molecules, particularly beneficial in drug-delivery systems. Its capacity to target intracellular components, such as mitochondria, renders it a crucial substance in biomedical research.

This paper seeks to examine the efficacy of chitosan-based nanoparticles for targeted medication delivery to mitochondria, particularly in the context of cancer treatment. The aim is to improve drug bioavailability, stability, and efficacy through the chemical modification of chitosan. The document additionally explores its extensive applications in gene therapy and vaccination administration.

The electrostatic characteristics of chitosan and its capacity to create diverse structures such as hydrogels and nanoparticles facilitate targeted medication release and enhanced cellular absorption. This biopolymer improves the specificity of cancer cells while reducing toxicity to healthy cells. The paper examines chitosan's interaction with cellular membranes and mitochondria to optimize therapeutic results.

Chitosan nanoparticles have considerable promise in the targeted delivery of drugs to mitochondria, especially in the context of cancer therapy. Their cationic properties enhance bioavailability; yet, issues related to solubility and safety require resolution. Further study is required to optimize these nanoparticles for safe and effective application in clinical environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan is a special polymer for use in the pharmaceutical and biomedical industries because of its non-toxicity, biological compatibility, and biodegradability. The late 1990s saw the first descriptions of the use of chitosan nanoparticles and microspheres for medication delivery (Calvo et al., 1997; 1997; Berthold et al., 1996). Chitosan is another molecule that is very versatile because of its positive charge at physiological pH 7.4, as shown by the protonation process of d-glucosamine in its crystalline structure and variable molecular mass (Ojeda-Hernández et al., 2020). It is made by deacetylating the acetyl groups in chitin, a naturally occurring polymer. The degree of deacetylation of chitosan determines both the chemical properties and the biological activity of the material. Other properties that influence the behavior of chitosan are size, which primarily revolves around the size of the chitosan molecules and any alterations done to them (Wang et al., 2020). Chitosan is resistant in water yet accessible in acidic solutions, possessing a pKa of 6.5. Hydroxyapatite (HAp), a calcium phosphate compound represented by the formula Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂, exhibits exceptional biocompatibility and is commonly employed in medicine delivery devices due to its similarity to bone mineral (Nayak et al., 2019 Jan, 1). When combined with chitosan, it offers structural stability and facilitates prolonged drug release, improving the targeted delivery of medicinal medicines to mitochondria. Likewise, additional calcium phosphate phases, like tricalcium phosphate (TCP) and dicalcium phosphate (DCP), enhance delivery methods by emulating intracellular calcium dynamics. These phases provide biodegradability and improve encapsulation efficiency, rendering them helpful in mitochondrial-targeting techniques (Bose & Tarafder, 2012 Apr, 1). Moreover, mesoporous silica NPs (MSNs) enhance these systems due to their extensive surface area and adjustable pore dimensions (Kankala et al., 2020, Jun), facilitating increased drug loading and regulated release. The integration of silica with chitosan improves intracellular absorption and guarantees targeted distribution to mitochondria while reducing cytotoxicity. These materials—hydroxyapatite, calcium phosphate phases, and silica collaborate with chitosan to construct sophisticated, biocompatible systems for effective and targeted mitochondrial drug delivery.

Through polyelectrolyte interactions, protonated species can associate with many anionic biomolecules, including microscopic particles like DNA, lipids, and proteins. Chitosan NP has been successfully employed in drug delivery applications for diverse payloads, such as insulin, anticancer drugs, and gene transfer, ranging from oral administration to systemic cancer therapy (Jhaveri et al., 2021). Size and charge, for example, have a significant role in determining how chitosan NPs and their derivatives interact biologically (Moraru et al., 2020; Fröhlich, 2017; Jiang et al., 2017). Because of its cationic nature, chitosan has mucoadhesive qualities that make it useful for ocular and intranasal medication administration. The cellular absorption of treatments like anticancer medicines and macromolecules such as proteins and genetic material has been boosted by chitosan NPs (Pilipenko et al., 2019; Nam & Nah, 2016; Chen et al., 2017, 2018). Due to its mucoadhesive and adjuvant characteristics, chitosan-based vaccines exhibit enhanced mucosal permeability along with stimulation of macrophages (Garg et al., 2019). The success of chitosan in transporting these therapeutic compounds depends on its fate at the cellular level. Therefore, the efficacy and safety of the therapeutic compounds that chitosan is meant to carry depend on how the chitosan itself is processed and eliminated from the body.

Consequently, understanding the properties of chitosan associations at the intracellular and cellular levels of tissues is essential for developing effective delivery techniques. The fabrication and application of chitosan NP have been thoroughly examined in several papers. Recent assessments of its biological effects and influence on cellular distribution have been enlightening and will facilitate the advancement of innovative formulations. This research examines the various characteristics that affect chitosan's engagement with the cellular membrane, then its internalization and translocation inside the cell, and its eventual exocytosis. The discussion also encompasses the toxicity and bioavailability in various tissues inside the body. Fig. 1 represents the target of the mitochondrial site via self-chitosanNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, characteristics, and applications of chitosan

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan, a naturally occurring cationic polysaccharide, is formed when chitin undergoes N-deacetylation. Chitin is the second most prevalent polymer on Earth after cellulose (Singh et al., 2017). With molecular weights ranging from 10 to >1000 kDa, it is a linear copolymer (Husain et al., 2017; Varlamov et al., 2020). The chemical structure of chitosan is comparable to that of glycosaminoglycan, which is the primary element of the extracellular matrix (Kim et al., 2018). For quality control, a variety of chitosan physicochemical parameters are frequently checked and evaluated (Detsi et al., 2020; Philibert et al., 2017). a variety of factors, including conductivity, permeability, bending strength, density, crystallization, deacetylation degree (DD), molecular weight, and the capacity to dissolve. Physical qualities of chitosan impact both its chemical and biological properties, with molecular weight and DD being two of the most critical factors (Foster et al., 2015; Chatelet et al., 2001; Tangsadthakun et al., 2007). There has been significant interest in the possibly advantageous pharmacological properties of chitosan formulations characterized by various degrees of deacetylation, molecular mass, and sequences of molecular derivatization. Alongside its superior polyelectrolyte functionality, the ability to degrade immunogenicity, biological activity, antibacterial efficacy, anticancer attributes, oxidative characteristics, cellular adhesion capabilities, low toxicity, and capacity for chemical manipulation (Augustine et al., 2020; Islam et al., 2016; Shariatinia, 2018; Fakhri et al., 2020), Chitosan also has several other notable biological properties. However, the utility of chitosan is considerably constrained by its inadequate solubility in the fluid and most organic solvents. It is mostly soluble in most moderate acidic solutions with a pH below 6.5. Improving the ability to dissolve chitosan is essential for identifying novel applications. The ability to dissolve of chitosan as well as its biological functions can be enhanced through several techniques, including deacetylation, and synthetic modification by integrating hydrophilic bioactive molecules into amino or hydroxyl groups (Wang et al., 2020). The medical and pharmaceutical sectors extensively utilize chitosan and its analogs owing to their many beneficial qualities, resulting in their application across various contexts, involving medication administration (Ali & Ahmed, 2018; Ahsan et al., 2018), tissue engineering (Preethi Soundarya et al., 2018), wound dressing (Bano et al., 2017; Park et al., 2018), genetic and oncological therapy (Fathi et al., 2018; Santos-Carballal et al., 2018; Chuan et al., 2019), antibiofilm agents (Khan et al., 2020).

The complicated structure of the cell membrane, which is made of proteins and lipids, prevents the bulk of chemicals from entering the cell. Drug molecules must first cross this barrier to start exerting therapeutic effects (Yang & Hinner, 2015). Mammalian cell plasma membranes are net negatively charged because they contain phospholipids with negative head groups (Magalhaes & Glogauer, 2010). Increased cellular absorption is made possible by the electrostatic interactions between the surface of cell membranes and a cationic polysaccharide, such as chitosan. Schipper et al. explored the early phases of cellular absorption of chitosan solutions with various molecular weights and acetylation levels. When chitosan's capability to promote absorption in Caco-2 cells was investigated, it was revealed that higher epithelial permeability to mannitol was caused by insufficient acetylation and probably a high molecular weight (Schipper et al., 1996). Kotzé et al. were among the initial researchers to indicate that chitosan may enhance peptide transport across Caco-2 cell monolayers by reducing transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) activity. Chitosan hydrochloride was found to have the highest effect in lowering the TEER when compared to chitosan glutamate and chitosan hydrochloride, as well as a chitosan derivative, trimethyl chitosan (Kotzé et al., 1997). In the subsequent parts, we will analyze in depth the most recent mechanistic studies of how chitosan influences cellular absorption. Fig. 2 shows the effect of chitosan to control release for the mitochondrial target.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of zeta potential and pH on cellular membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Yue et al. explored the impact of chitosan nanoparticles on the cell membrane by evaluating their physical and chemical characteristics. They studied how efficiently these NPs were absorbed by eight distinct cell lines, including skin, blood vessels, connective tissue, and blood cells. According to the researchers, chitosan nanoparticles' positive surface charge boosted their sensitivity to cellular absorption. The NPs and cell membranes have a significant electrostatic contact, which explains the fast and efficient absorption. These positively charged NPs possessed a zeta potential of +39.25 ± 2.68 mV because, in contrast to neutral or negative NPs, they were strongly electrostatically attracted to the cell membranes (Yue et al., 2011). Even with the inclusion of an external change, chitosan's potential to increase cellular absorption remained intact, while the NPs' positive charge was retained. When gold nanoparticles were treated with chitosan, the resultant particles possessed a zeta potential of +65 ± 1.0 mV and cationic characteristics. When such particles were employed to pretreat human monocytic THP-1 cells, internalization happened quickly within 30 min whereas when negatively charged particles were utilized, pretreatment took six hours (Boyles et al., 2015). FITC-BSA and positively charged chondroitin sulfate chitosan nanoparticles (NPs) with a zeta potential of +16 mV demonstrated higher cellular absorption than their inversely charged counterparts. Compared to negatively charged particles, increased fluorescence levels two hours after incubation showed enhanced absorption of positively charged components (Hu et al., 2012). The surface charge of chitosan NPs is significantly influenced by the pH of their environment. Chitosan might be soluble at acidic pH (pKa = 6.5) due to the protonation process of amino groups, which increases the electrical charge on the surface of the NPs (Kalliola et al., 2018; Nogueira et al., 2013). Numerous NPs have been engineered to enhance drug delivering to tumors by utilizing chitosan's capacity for targeted drug releasing within the target cellular milieu (Amoozgar et al., 2012; Khalili & Ahmad, 2015). Upon transition from a healthy pH of 7.2 to a tumor microenvironment pH of 6.8, pH-responsive NPs utilizing self-assemblage polymers composed of dimethyl maleic acid, chitosan, and urocanic acid with doxorubicin exhibited a charge reversing characteristic. When the zeta potential was negative at body temperature and pH, the particles were more stable in the blood and less likely to be adsorbed by proteins that weren't intended to be there. However, when the tumor microenvironment flipped to a positive zeta potential, Doxorubicin NPs were internalized by cells with greater effectiveness and liberated more rapidly. In a second paper, an intelligent pH-responsive chitosan coating was used to target the anticancer drug paclitaxel specifically to tumor cells. PLGA nanoparticles with lower molecular weight chitosan of MW 50–190 kDa had greater interactions with SKOV-3 and NCI/ADR-RES human ovarian cancer cells at slightly acidic pH 6.2 compared with normal pH 7.2, whose interactions were attenuated (Chen et al., 2012). Chitosan's pH sensitivity enables it to selectively target distinct microbial environments within and surrounding cells, hence improving its association with cells and facilitating improved absorption into cells. The mechanism by which mitochondria-targeted nanoparticles (T-ZnPc-NPs) trigger cell death and immune activation. Upon light activation at 660 nm, the nanoparticles generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) within the mitochondria, leading to both apoptosis and necrosis. Immature dendritic cells (DCs) then engulf these dying cells, leading to their maturation. The mature DCs present tumor antigens on their surface, activating T cells and potentially initiating an immune response against tumor cells. The nanoparticles are synthesized using a polymer matrix that encapsulates zinc phthalocyanine, enabling their targeted delivery to mitochondria for photodynamic therapy as shown in Fig. 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Function of Chitosan in cell adhesion
ce:section.ce:para: Cells may cling to static substrates like culture dishes and Petri plates owing to particular membrane receptor binding activities that facilitate mechanical interaction between intracellular actin and the extracellular matrix. Without the use of enzyme treatments, chitosan membranes may be utilized to separate cultured cells from the substrate (Noriega & Subramanian, 2011). When exposed to pH fluctuations, various cell lines including HeLa, primary corneal fibroblasts, and NIH-3T3 were shown to detach from their surroundings. Cell detachment is exacerbated by high pH because it causes chitosan to deprotonate, which encourages fibronectin to desorb from the substrate (Noriega & Subramanian, 2011). Recently, chitosan's charge-dependent interaction with cell membranes has been exploited to modify the behavior of cell adhesion. In the pH range of 3.0 to 5.0, researchers observed that the PC-3 prostate cancer line of cells exhibited better adherence to hyaluronic acid-chitosan films (Lü et al., 2018). Bovine chondrocytes adhered to chitosan films more easily when they were neutralized with sodium hydroxide (Lim et al., 2007). Cell growth and adhesion to the scaffolds varied as a result. The higher fibronectin absorption on collagen-chitosan-coated surfaces encouraged greater PC12 cell attachment (Foster et al., 2015). Variations in hydrophobicity as well as the number of protonated units accessible for adhesion to cells are correlated with the extent of chitosan acetylation, which subsequently influences its effects on cell adherence as well as reproduction potential. Chitosan-based films that underwent acetylation demonstrated enhanced hydrophobicity and reduced interfacial charge, leading to decreased adherence of fibroblast as well as chondrocyte cells (Rodríguez-Vázquez et al., 2015). Similarly, sensory epithelial cells additionally exhibited enhanced cell adaptability as well as proliferative behaviors at a deacetylation level reaching eighty-five percent, indicating that decreased acetylation of chitosan was advantageous for cell proliferation as well as adherence (Boecker et al., 2019). Frameworks for the regeneration of cartilage and tendons, as well as the regeneration of peripheral nerves (Dixit et al., 2017), dermatological grafting (Salati et al., 2011; Vijayan et al., 2019), tissue repair (Liu et al., 2018; Cho et al., 2016), and Three-dimensional cellular cultures (Kafi et al., 2020; Yeh et al., 2011) are just some examples of the many ways in which chitosan's ability to promote cell adhesion has been put to use in the field of tissue engineering (Fig. 4 ).

Hydrophilic CS derivatives are suitable drug carriers because they are mucoadhesive, biocompatible, and rapidly absorbed in the intestines. Since CS by itself is difficult for drug distribution, medicines are commonly coupled to hydrophobic molecules with a biocompatible outer layer. Processes like Schiff-base and N-acylation may immobilize hydrophobic molecules on CS, including cholesterol, natural lipids, and hydrophobic medicines. The CS-based conjugates then induce self-assembly, leading to the creation of nanomaterials.

Cheng et al. started with the TMC and proceeded to amidate it using oleyl-NHS. Amphipathic polymers, including oleyl-conjugated trimethyl chitosan (OTMC), are self-assembled to allow targeted gene delivery. Self-assembled NPs of amphipathic CS derivatives coated in gold nanospheres were able to create excellent tumor-targeted photothermal therapy. Gan et al. loaded the insulin onto the NPs by treating CS with deoxycholic acid to produce DNPs. By imitating the bile acid system, NPs treated with deoxycholic acid may be able to pass through the intestinal epithelium. The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has certified PLA as a safe hydrophobic biodegradable polyester. In this design, the OLA encapsulated the hydrophobic drug lidocaine as an inner hydrophobic layer.

Cross-linking occurs when at least two compounds are connected chemically by covalent bonds (Prabaharan & Mano, 2008). Organic dibasic acid and polyhydric alcohol are two examples of crosslinkers possessing many functional groups that are needed for covalent cross-linking. Crosslinkers such as genipin Laroui et al. (2010) and glutaraldehyde (Ding et al., 2017) establish bridge connections between the various polymer chains, enabling nanomaterials to take on a three-dimensional shape. The three-dimensional structure of nanomaterials created by covalent cross-linking was found to be substantially more stable. CS/poly(acrylic acid) nanocapsules containing antibiotics and smaller than 100 nm in size were created by Carbonnier et al. (Belbekhouche et al., 2018). Electrochemical deposition of chitosan and PLA over gold NPs generated using sulfonated thiol groups as sacrificial templates. Similar mechanisms of diffusion and electrostatic interactions were used to load the beta-lactam antibiotic amoxicillin. Subsequently, the cyanide hydrolyzed the gold core. Last but not least, glutaraldehyde was used to make capsules that were cross-linked using the Schiff-base formation method. The dispersion, concentration, and hollow size of the nanocapsules may be modulated due to the existence of gold NPs and the significant barrier created by the numerous polyelectrolyte bilayers.

The prodigiosin-encapsulated CS microspheres developed by Soboyejo et al. (Dozie-Nwachukwu et al., 2017) are intended for targeted medication delivery. The anti-cancer capabilities of prodigiosin come from a secondary metabolite produced by Serratia marcescens. Cross-linking with glutaraldehyde was crucial in the preparation of the prodigiosin-encapsulated chitosan microspheres. The effectiveness of the encapsulation was directly proportional to the drug/polymer ratio. Based on the data collected thus far, CS nanospheres containing integrated prodigiosin proved effective in eliminating the mammary carcinoma cell line.

Chitosan is a naturally occurring carbohydrate with polycationic properties that allow it to generate NPs by gelling with certain polyanionic chemicals or negatively charged molecules (Canepa et al., 2017). It is the cross-links that are responsible for the gelation process between the polyanions and the cationic chitosan. Chitosan's favorable properties have led to its utilization in the creation of drug delivery systems. An anionic molecule called sodium tripolyphosphate (TPP) was one of the first and is presently commonly exploited for ionic crosslinking with CS. It was originally described for use in medication delivery by Bodmeier et al. (Prabaharan & Mano, 2008) in 1989. After completing a complete statistical investigation of the important parameters determining the creation of NP, Moerschbacher et al. (Sreekumar et al., 2018) Throughout the polymerization process, the molecular ratio of chitosan (CS) to tripolyphosphate (TPP), the solvent conditions, and the CS concentration may all be varied to alter the size of CS NPs. Mao et al. (Zhang et al., 2018) designed a core-shell nanocomposite material that can penetrate intestinal mucus to inject insulin orally. Ionic crosslinking between polycationic chitosan and tripolyphosphate generated the core, which was subsequently surrounded by a unique layer made by electrostatic attraction. Insulin was utilized as a medical medication. Since most viruses have both hydrophilic and neutral surfaces, which make it easier for them to propagate through mucus, these researchers designed a novel form of nanoparticle that can permeate mucus. Moreover, anionic macromolecules such as hyaluronic acid and alginate may potentially react with cationic CS via electrolyte contact to form drug-delivery nanoparticles. A nanomaterial created by Tirelli et al. was focused on synergistic dendritic cells (DC). Hyaluronic acid (HA) links itself to a surface receptor termed CD44, which is involved in T-cell activation, adhesion, and DC maturation. Drug delivery nanocomposites are formed when the anionic glycosaminoglycan (GAG) HA combines with the cationic CS. Moreover, conjugates of HA as well as mannose enhance the absorption of nanocomposites by dendritic cells, partly due to mannose's attraction for the extracellular lectin. To target dendritic cells for delivery, they synthesized mannosylated HA-CS nanocomposites using polyelectrolyte complexity (Fig. 6 ).

To manage endoparasites, Mertins et al. (Mathews et al., 2018) produced a pH-sensitive nanovehicle by combining anionic alginate with a swirling mixture of deacetylated chitosan. The biological efficiency of the nanocarrier was assessed in fish by administering the anthelmintic medication to them. A core-shell NP for a sequentially programmed DR system was created by Chu et al. exploiting the polyelectrolyte interaction between anionic PLGA and CS (Yang et al., 2016). A lipophilic PLGA core and a hydrophilic CS shell were produced from the medication. A limited amount of the drug is released fast initially because of its sensitivity to stomach acid. The leftover drug was steadily released by the hydrophobic polylactic acid core, which assisted the body's natural healing process. The molecule that is ionically cross-linked to the anionic polymeric material chitosan may be replaced with PAA and other anionic polymers that operate via polyelectrolyte linkages, including 6-phosphogluconic trisodium salt and poly-c-glutamic acid (Yang et al., 2016).

Numerous inorganic nanoparticles are being created for drug delivery owing to their remarkable and easily regulated physical and chemical features (Assa et al., 2017). The porosity of mesoporous silica (Gupta et al., 2018), the photosensitive fever and luminescence of gold nanoparticles, the magnetic responsiveness of iron oxide, and the luminescence of carbon quantum dots all assist in improving the delivery of pharmaceuticals and the detection of disorders. Their low stability, possibility for non-targeting side effects, and modification hurdles have greatly hampered their application in the biomedical profession, despite their exciting promise (Fulop et al., 2018). An inorganic nanoparticle core and a multifunctional surface coating make up these inorganic NPs employed in drug delivery systems (DDS). Because of its superior characteristics, CS is a suitable replacement for inorganic NP decoration. Because of their outstanding chemical stability, mesoporous architecture, and biological compatibility, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) are interesting alternatives for drug delivery. McNally et al. created a dual-targeting mesoporous silica nanoparticle for cancer therapy (Gurka et al., 2016).

Mesoporous silica served as the NP's core, while a CS shell encasing a urokinase plasminogen activator (UPA) ligand provided the NP's outside coating. The mesoporous architecture of the silica core enhanced drug extraction; UPA, a lipid with a high sensitivity for the urokinase plasminogen activation recipient, is markedly overexpressed in pancreatic cancer; and CS functions as pH-sensitive medication-releasing agents in a highly acidic tumor milieu. The integrated nanoparticle demonstrated excellent outcomes when utilized for targeted medication delivery to pancreatic cancer cells. Magnetic NPs (MNPs), characterized by elevated magnetization values, can target a drug to the afflicted region when subjected to an external magnetic field in a DDS. Derived from intelligent multifunctional magnetic NPs (Hua et al., 2017), Ji et al. created a DDS that integrates cancer thermo-chemotherapy as well as subcellular surveillance as its twin functions. Following the incorporation of the drug 5-FU and the metal nanoparticle (MNP) Fe3O4 into the mesoporous SiO2 core, the entire assembly was enveloped in a shell composed of the thermosensitive polymer PNIPAM as well as cationic chitosan (CS). The medicine is released most efficiently from the smart delivery vehicle when the temperature inside the tumor is 45 °Celsius. Furthermore, fluorescent molecules were attached to nanocarriers for use in imaging cells. In terms of long-term drug release and illness diagnostics, the multi-responsive drug carriers have shown striking advantages. Photo-absorbing agents like gold nanospheres can convert the thermal energy of visible light into a usable form for photothermal anticancer treatment. In this case, the TNYL served as a targeting molecule with a strong affinity for EpHB4 receptors on tumor cells. This treatment selectively targeted EphB4-positive tumors and showed striking anticancer activity when applied in conjunction with NIR laser irradiation. Carbon quantum dots (CQD), characterized by their distinctive optical properties such as excellent biological compatibility, diminutive size, affordability, and near-infrared (NIR) absorption as well as emission capabilities, have been extensively researched as innovative nanomaterials for treatment with photothermal energy as well as NIR fluorescence imaging in biomedical fields. The integration of pH-sensitive chitosan with luminescent carbon dots into a cohesive nanostructure for synergistic photothermal therapy was detailed, and cellular as well as in vivo evaluations verified that carbon dot hybrid nanogels (CCHNs) are safe for application in live organisms. It exhibited intelligent release of drugs in response to both near-infrared (NIR) light and pH alterations, and its fluorescence was sufficiently stable for concurrent NIR imaging. Interestingly, Wu et al. (Xu et al., 2016). Employed hydrothermal combination of carbon source, ethylenediamine, as well as mercaptosuccinic acid to synthesize a luminous carbon quantum dot that specifically targets mitochondria. Synthesized CQDs exhibited advantageous characteristics including affordability, straightforward synthesis, superior mitochondria-targeting particularity, extended photographic stability, and favorable surface-altering capabilities. A plethora of newly synthesized inorganic compounds, including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Hidalgo et al., 2017) and layered double hydroxides (LDH) (Xu et al., 2016), collaborated with CS to build DDSs (Table 2 ).

The principal process for transferring charged biomolecules across the cell membrane is active endocytosis (Foroozandeh & Aziz, 2018). To eliminate chitosan NPs (NPs), the major method for chitosan transport requires both phagocytosis and pinocytosis. Caveolin-mediated, cadherin-mediated, and clathrin-mediated pinocytosis are examples of uptake mechanisms. Particles bigger than 250 nm are phagocytosed, while chitosan is absorbed by cells via pinocytosis, which relies on the particle's size, charge, and surface modifications. The earliest phases of clathrin-mediated endocytosis involve internalization and inclusion into late endosomes, which enable cargo to transit to lysosomes (Li et al., 2017). Caveolae-mediated endocytosis, which employs the GTPase dynamin to internalize things via plasma membranes and transfer cargo to lysosomes, is made simpler by cholesterol-rich, flask-shaped incursions in the plasma membrane (Li et al., 2017; Juliano & Carver, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of charge and dimensions on the uptake mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The degree to which cells absorb chitosan NP varies on a variety of criteria, including size, cell line, and experiment. Greater quantities of chitosan-coated PLGA NPs (up to 200 nm) were absorbed by A549 cells, demonstrating that clathrin mediates the energy-dependent endocytic process (Malatesta et al., 2015). The absorption of chitosan particles packed with ovalbumin and FITC-bovine serum albumin by bone marrow-derived dendritic cells and the RAW 264.7 murine macrophage cell line indicated that uptake was based on particle size rather than processing time or quantity. Macrophages and dendritic cells received more particles in the 1 µm range than in the 300 nm or 3 µm range (Huang et al., 2002). However, when exposed to FITC-labeled chitosan nanoparticles, which were roughly 250 nm in size, macrophages displayed diminished phagocytic ability. Small chitosan nanoparticles (NPs) in the 25 nm range were readily internalized by L929 fibroblast cells, however bigger NPs up to 150 nm required more energy to be absorbed in a sodium azide environment. For endocytic processes, it has been demonstrated that both caveoli and lipid rafts are sources of both nanoparticle sizes (Park & Cho, 2007). The preferred internalization of cationic NPs by non-phagocytic cells is controlled by both charge density and hydrophobicity, according to the charge-dependent absorption pathway. Although there is no hard-and-fast rule for this, clathrin-mediated endocytosis is the predominant mechanism exploited by both cationic and anionic chitosan NPs (Tahara et al., 2009). Rhodamine-labeled carboxymethyl chitosan NPs and chitosan hydrochloride-grafted NPs, measuring 300 and 500 nm, were examined for their phagocytic and non-phagocytic absorption by murine peritoneal macrophages. The phagocytic uptake of positively charged particles exceeded that of those with negatively charged particles. Analogous to phagocytic absorption, non-phagocytic uptake preferentially favored positively charged NPs over negatively charged ones; however, the extent of absorption was significantly influenced by the specific cell line employed. Moreover, a significant decrease in absorption was seen at 4 °C, indicating that the uptake mechanism was very energy-dependent. Cellular absorption routes facilitated by clathrin as well as caveolae were also prominent (Koppolu & Zaharoff, 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The function of hydrophilia and hydrophobicity in the process of absorption
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrophobic chains coupled to polymers improve their cell absorption, gene transport, and cancer cell penetration (Tammam et al., 2015; Fröhlich, 2012). The greater absorption is mainly attributed to increased hydrophobic interactions with the lipid cell membranes; however, cellular absorption also proceeds via many mechanisms, including those mediated by caveolin and clathrin. Glycol chitosan hydrophobic modifications were employed by Nam et al. to distribute the chemical precisely to tumors. Micropinocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and caveolae-mediated endocytosis were the three ways by which hydrophobically modified chitosan nanoparticles (NPs), with an average size of 310 nm, were cellularly absorbed in HeLa cells. All three methods exhibited an additive impact. According to the results of our research, all of these routes were functioning (He et al., 2010). Non-clathrin-mediated absorption was found in hydrophobically modified N-palmitoyl chitosan with an average particle size of roughly 200 nm at lower hydrophobic displacement levels; at higher displacement levels, macropinocytosis took over as the predominant uptake mechanism. NP hydrophobicity increased negatively with caveolae-mediated absorption, demonstrating that lipid raft-mediated caveolae were the preferred route at high hydrophobicity (Liu et al., 2010).To successfully transmit genes to HEK 293 cells, chitosan was treated with hexanoic acid as well as monomethoxy-PEG to create pDNA polyplexes. Clathrin-mediated cellular uptake was predominant for these polyplexes, which had a circumference of below 200 nm along with a positive zeta potential (Nam et al., 2009). Comparable outcomes were seen when chitosan underwent hydrophobic modification with linoleic acid as well as hydrophilic modification with poly(-malic acid), resulting in a bilaterally transplanted chitosan formic complex with pDNA (Chiu et al., 2010; Layek et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent advancements in the pharmaceutical industries in the use of nanomaterials derived from Chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: These disorders require strong therapeutic approaches because they disrupt normal bodily functions (Sun et al., 2018). The ideal drug should therefore be risk-free, efficient, reliable, and accurate. The design of pharmaceutical delivery systems should be informed by the chemical and physical properties of the drugs. Methods for creating drug-loaded NPs have been used that make use of both natural and synthetic compounds. Nano drugs can directly interact with the lesion and be progressively injected into cells because of their wide coverage area. Drug release sensitivity is increased and targeted delivery to particular cells or tissues is made possible by conjugating nanomaterials with different functional groups (De Campos et al., 2004). NPs are created in the interim using strategies designed to get around the difficulties involved in transporting medications. The CS DDSs are shown in Fig. 7 . Chitosan's cationic (positively charged) nature is key to its effectiveness, as it enables chitosan nanoparticles (NPs) to preferentially accumulate in mitochondria, particularly in cancer cells. Cancerous cells typically exhibit a higher mitochondrial membrane potential compared to healthy cells, making them more receptive to positively charged particles like chitosan NPs. This charge-based attraction allows chitosan NPs to localize within the mitochondria, where they can disrupt the mitochondrial membrane, release cytochrome c, and initiate apoptosis—a process particularly advantageous in inducing cancer cell death while minimizing impact on normal cells.

Additionally, chitosan-based NPs play a crucial role in reactive oxygen species (ROS) production within mitochondria, leveraging ROS to induce cell death specifically in cancer cells. The elevated ROS within cancerous mitochondria creates a positive feedback loop, amplifying mitochondrial stress and leading to cell apoptosis. By focusing ROS production within the mitochondria, chitosan enhances the therapeutic efficacy of treatments, using cancer cells’ mitochondrial vulnerabilities as a targeted point of intervention. Chitosan's biocompatibility and versatility further enhance its utility in targeted drug delivery systems. This material can carry both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs, offering flexibility for a wide range of therapeutic agents. However, challenges remain, such as ensuring selective targeting to avoid impacting healthy mitochondria and balancing ROS production to maximize therapeutic benefits without excessive cytotoxicity. Overall, chitosan's unique properties make it a promising material for targeted mitochondrial therapies, particularly in oncology, with ongoing research focusing on refining its safety and specificity for clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Chitosan in pharmaceutical delivery systems

ce:section.ce:para: Oral administration (OD) is safe, inexpensive, and well-received by patients, it is the recommended form of administration for new medication studies. (Samanta et al., 2017) A drug enters the gastrointestinal system and is absorbed into the blood via the cells of the epithelium lining the tract. The medicine can then continue to therapeutically alter the body's organs and tissues. Nevertheless, OD isn't always the optimal solution as some medications may be irritated by the GIT or destroyed by stomach acid (Situ et al., 2018). Consequently, OD medications must be constant and devoid of gastrointestinal adverse effects. Produced by beta cells in the pancreas, the protein hormone insulin boosts the synthesis of proteins, lipids, and glycogen while reducing blood glucose levels. One or more times a day, patients commonly inject insulin subcutaneously (SC), which may injure the skin and impair the healing process for diabetes (Shrestha et al., 2016). Although delivering insulin orally is greatly desirable, there are significant obstacles. The enormous molecular weight and hydrophilicity of insulin make it difficult for it to penetrate through biofilms. Insulin may be promptly neutralized by stomach acids. To better diabetes therapy, researchers are devoted to creating an oral insulin delivery method. Ionic interactions enable insulin, an anionic protein, to readily connect with CS. Using electrostatic complexation, Chen et al. created a homogeneous core-shell NP to administer insulin via OD. Thiolated hyaluronic acid (HA-SH) functions as an anionic shell, and a positively charged core was generated by mixing insulin and chitosan (HTCC) with N-(2‑hydroxy-propyl)−3-trimethylammonium chloride (Liu et al., 2016). This insulin administration approach for diabetes, substantiated by in vitro and ex vivo studies, utilized particles averaging 100 nm in size and showed high effectiveness in encapsulation along with loading ability. Anderson et al. (Gu et al., 2013) developed glucose-sensitive closed-loop insulin nanocapsules for injectable delivery. Electrostatic complexation assembled a pH-sensitive chitosan matrix, glucose-specific enzymes, and insulin to create the nanocapsules. The enzymatic transformation of glucose toward gluconic acid and CS-mediated protonation process would result in the enlargement of the microgel system, hence enhancing the rate of insulin production under hyperglycemic circumstances. Studies utilizing mouse models of type 1 diabetes demonstrate that the simultaneous pH, as well as enzymatic responsiveness carrier, effectively modulates blood glucose levels.

Gastric cancer initially occurs in the inner stomach lining, known as gastric mucosa. (-)-Epigallocatechin-3-gallate, a constituent of green tea, exerts its anti-tumor effect by anti-angiogenesis, that is, blocking the formation of blood vessels, cell proliferation, and apoptosis; or death of tumor cells. The problem here is that EGCG is poorly absorbed from the stomach lining and readily degraded in the gastrointestinal tract, thereby EGCG cannot be used for treating gastric carcinoma. Fucose, an effective sugar, may improve the therapy as it may regulate crucial biological processes. It can facilitate chitosan that adheres to the mucus lining of the gastrointestinal tract could increase the absorption of EGCG and PEG which prolongs the residence time of a treatment in the bloodstream. In vitro and in vivo laboratory and animal tests were performed on nanoparticles with EGCG compared to treatments without EGCG. Furthermore, Santos et al. (Sun et al., 2021 Oct, 6) engineered a versatile, customizable composite structure based on mucoadhesive chitosan to deliver peptide medicines orally (Tang et al., 2018).

Injectables are often administered using intravenous (IV), cutaneous, intramuscular, and intra-arterial channels. By directly injecting medications into the circulatory system, intravenous administration allows for quick and efficient systemic circulation entrance. Injections administered intramuscularly or subcutaneously have limited bioavailability and delayed effectiveness since they must pass through biofilm layers before they can access the bloodstream. Arterial injection is most often used for blood transfusions. To treat chitosan nanodrugs, intravenous injection administration is required. Doxorubicin is released on demand using stepwise pH-responsive NPs developed by Zeng et al. (Tang et al., 2018). Because doxorubicin-loaded NPs (NPs) included pH-responsive dimethylmaleic acid and urocanic acid, they responded gradually to changes in both intracellular and extracellular pH levels after being injected into tumor-bearing rats. Tumour tissues in the somewhat acidic extracellular milieu of the tumor exhibited a notable absorption of the DOX-loaded NPs. In the acidic endo/lysosomal environment, the DOX was subsequently released upon interest from NPs. The doxorubicin was collected in tumor tissue by the stepwise pH-responsive NPs more quickly and over a longer time than by free doxorubicin. For the sequential release of DOX via tail vein injection, they also created NPs that are pH-/reduction-responsive (Caramella et al., 2015). Yan and colleagues (Chen et al., 2017) developed CS-modified selenium nanoparticles (SC) to deliver peptides generated from TNF-α, therefore enhancing their anticancer efficacy. The TNF-α-derived peptide P16 (SCP) was conjugated to SC, the slow-release carrier, in the NPs. Prostate cancer cells, particularly DU145 cells, were significantly inhibited in their ability to proliferate after being injected into the tail vein. While disulphide linkages were used to join chitosan and polyethyleneimine (PEI), ureocanic acid was covalently bonded to the polymer utilizing amide bonds. Toll-like receptor 4 siRNA (TLR4siRNA) and DOX were then combined into a micelle and delivered by the tail vein; this combination has shown promising results in tumor treatment.

The fact that injections function more quickly for severe or acute illnesses makes them a clear advantage in emergency settings. However, there are potential risks associated with injectable delivery, such as vascular injury, skin damage, and significant bacterial and viral infection. As a result, research towards safer, more efficient, and more complex delivery systems is essential (Table 3 ).

Conventional drug delivery techniques, including oral or injectable, may result in unintended systemic adverse effects; topical treatment may avoid this issue (Kwon et al., 2017). The illness site may be reached considerably more quickly since it can readily penetrate the epidermis and other mucosal membranes (Mohammed et al., 2017). The likelihood of topical therapy is higher for diseases that appear in mucosa-rich regions such as the mouth, rectum, urethra, vagina, bladder, eyes, and nasopharynx (De la Fuente et al., 2010). The ability of CS, a naturally occurring cationic glycan, to adhere to mucosa has been extensively used in the development of innovative nanomaterials for topical medication delivery. To treat eye conditions, active ingredients are often used topically. The eye's innate defensive system has, however, made treatment more difficult. Metabolic enzymes found in ocular tissues aid in the breakdown of medications. The change improved both the vehicle's solubility and performance. As a thiol chemical, cysteine may create disulfide connections with both molecules and tissues, increasing the drug's tissue-based persistence. Some sulfhydryl residues in proteins and mucus on tissue surfaces might react with the thiol groups in the modified chitosan vehicle. The adhesion and precorneal retention were enhanced by the additional thiol group, and CS served as a crucial mucoadhesive component (De la Fuente et al., 2010).
This is an effective method that increases the amount of time the medicine spends in contact with the cornea to improve the delivery of diclofenac for ocular disorders.

Nasal administration is a viable choice for topical therapy in cases of antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, or antibacterial nasal obstruction, or hemostasis treatment (Lupo et al., 2017). Significantly, research indicates that the blood-brain barrier can be traversed, facilitating the direct delivery of pharmaceuticals from the nasal cavity to the brain Ahmad et al. (2017). Intranasal medication is simpler as well as more probable to be adhered to by patients relative to other pathways, as it does not necessitate any incisions. Chemically cross-linked chitosan microparticles were formulated for nasal administration by Katsarov et al. (Katsarov et al., 2018). The adsorption capacity of mucin was evaluated after particles were produced by covalently crosslinking CS with varying amounts of glutaraldehyde. The drug release period was greatly increased and in vitro testing showed that all of the particles had a high affinity for the mucin. The mucoadhesive CS-based microsphere is an exciting new nasal administration delivery technology.

The gut is home to a vast range of bacteria, all of which generate enzymes that breakdown materials with particular structures, such as polysaccharides having glycosidic linkages (Ahmad et al., 2017). -(1 4)-linked GlcNAc and GlcN are unevenly scattered with CS, a high-affinity substrate for gut flora digestion. CS is therefore a feasible way to accurately deliver drugs to the colon. The ability of two CS-based carriers to transport the drug (OSCS) was evaluated: N-naphthyl-N, O-succinyl chitosan (NSCS), and N-octyl-N, O-succinyl chitosan (NOSC). CUR was loaded using dialysis, a physical trapping approach. Micelle form altered considerably in each medium, and it was discovered that the pH influenced the releasing characteristics of CUR. The pH levels of the gastrointestinal tract were revealed to greatly enhance CUR secretion. Interestingly, they changed the 5TR1 DNA aptamer on the surface of the nanomaterial to boost its anti-tumor activity. The pH-sensitive, customized nanoparticle provides higher therapeutic advantages. They enclosed the proteins in granules and crosslinked them to retain their functioning during storage and ingestion. The capacity of the particles to target the colon preferentially was established by the analysis of particle form, protein loading, and release under diverse situations.

In carcinomas, cell proliferation is typically unregulated. Chemotherapy and surgical excision are typically used simultaneously to remove the malignancy. As a systemic treatment, chemotherapy also offers the benefit of eradicating any probable metastatic lesions that might have remained after surgery. The potential benefit of treating numerous lesions concurrently is clear, but there is also a danger of systemic side effects that can impact healthy tissue (Oshi et al., 2018; Ye et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). Numerous optimization strategies for chemotherapy have been developed in an attempt to tackle these challenges. It is feasible to tailor nanomaterials to interact with particular tissues or environs. CS's mucoadhesiveness and structural variation have led to its adaption to antitumor nano vehicles for the treatment of cancer.

DOX was covalently grafted onto TMC using cis-aconitic anhydride, and the resulting nano complex exhibited pH-dependent DOX release. Electrostatic adsorption was used to bind IL-2 to NPs without altering the cytokine's bioactivity. The nutrient folic acid (FA) has also been placed onto a nanocarrier for efficient distribution. The sub-spherical, positively charged nano complexes typically measure around 200 nm in diameter. The anticancer efficacy and in vivo size effects were both enhanced by the optimal combination therapy method.

To treat tumors, Shen et al. (Tan et al., 2017) developed nanospheres containing both DOX and Roussin's black salt (RBS), a photosensitive NO Among up-conversion luminescence matrix materials, NaYF4:Er upconversion NPs (UCNPs) are the most effective at being excited by low-energy light and emitting high-energy light. N-hydroxysuccinimide was used to attach oleoyl-CS to the capped UCNPs, which contained an active carboxylic acid with a long chain. DOX is released from the nanospheres as they swell in response to changes in pH. The encapsulated UCNPs and RBS facilitate NO discharge, and their complementary therapeutic impact demonstrates this as a useful technique for cancer treatment. After loading TPT into the MSN core, PAA and CS were used as the shell, and QT was attached to the TPT's outer surface. Once integrated drug-containing NPs were coupled to arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (cRGD), they were able to target cancer cells through integrin receptor-mediated endocytosis. The breakdown of CS and subsequent changes in pH across tissues and cells caused drug release. Treatment of breast cancer with this method is successful.

Numerous studies have demonstrated that chitosan-based NPs can facilitate the innovative delivery of anticancer pharmaceuticals (Tan et al., 2017). Efficient medication delivery methods for anti-cancer therapy are developed based on response to the environment as well as tailoring principles. Also, photodynamic and hyperthermia treatment combinations were planned when developing the drug delivery vectors.

The disease has been related to genetic mutations in genes that code proteins and are involved in numerous physiological activities. One possible field of medicine is the use of gene therapy to treat hereditary problems. To fix genetic abnormalities, "gene therapy" entails replacing a damaged gene with a working one that has been given. However, the body's enzymes and acids can break down DNA and RNA molecules. Because they are anionic polymers, DNA and RNA have a significant affinity for cationic polymers like CS (Fathi et al., 2018).

A double-stranded RNA of 20–25 nucleotides in length, small interfering RNA (siRNA) plays a wide variety of roles in biology (Cosco et al., 2015). Primarily, it has a special function in the RNA interference process, which controls gene expression in a predetermined way. The application of siRNA technology has expanded to many other fields of medicine, particularly those dealing with cancer and inflammation. Due to its poor stability, which is impacted by a large number of nucleases in vivo, it is rarely employed in the clinic (Shen et al., 2017).

CS-deoxycholic acid nanodroplets were engineered to encapsulate magnetic NPs for siRNA delivery. Initially, they modified the chitosan using perfluoropropane, subsequently with iron oxide, and ultimately, they covalently attached deoxycholic acid to the chitosan. The electrostatic bonds involving a cationic chitosan matrix along with the siRNA on the particle exterior facilitated their amalgamation. At 37 °C in serum, the combination of siRNA and NPs exhibited stability for a duration of up to 4 h. Iron oxide-containing NPs can be guided to the appropriate location by a foreign magnetic effect, while nanodroplets, aided by ultrasound, will experience a phase transition from liquid to microbubble state, resulting in increased volume as well as energy, thus dispensing the encapsulated siRNA (Gao et al., 2018). Ultrasound's therapy efficacy was positively connected with the intensity of these emissions. The tumor-suppressor p53 is a protein with an uneven distribution that has been proven to respond to cellular stressors. Using Massart's technique, the MNPs were prepared. The thymine and MNPs were prepared independently, and then CS was precipitated to create the TIPs. A strong affinity for cationic CS was observed for the thymine and telomeric DNA sequence (Hao et al., 2014). With the help of electrostatic interaction with cationic CS, siRNA was loaded. RGD peptide analogs served as the targeting function, and PEG detached from the nanocarriers to allow for cellular entrance. In rheumatoid arthritis, the signaling receptor Notch1 plays a crucial role (RA). As part of their research into anti-inflammatory effects, Kim et al. (Kim et al., 2015) created siRNA delivery NPs (siRNA-NPs) that specifically target the protein Notch1.

Any biological agent used to prevent or limit the spread of sickness is referred to as a vaccination. Live bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites; human or animal serum or cells; or enzymes could be utilized to manufacture the vaccine. The introduction of vaccinations has considerably benefited the struggle against sickness and its spread. However, there are several ways that the vaccine's quality is compromised during manufacture, storage, and delivery (Kim et al., 2015). Strict quality control methods must be maintained throughout vaccine production owing to the hazard that defective vaccines pose to human health. As a revolutionary sort of vaccine delivery technique, vaccinations protected by a nanomaterial shell have immense promise. Because of its low toxicity, low immunogenicity, biocompatibility, and biodegradability, CS is a viable choice for vaccine administration. Here, we shall cover gene delivery research based on computer science.

Tumor cell lysates may behave as antigens, inducing an immune response from dendritic cells. Mannose (Man) was added to chitosan to attract dendritic cells (DCs), and TCL (Man-CTS-TCL NPs) was loaded onto it to stimulate an immunological response. In a vaccine viability experiment, the potential of Man-CTS-TCL NPs (NPs) as a cancer vaccine was tested both in vitro and in vivo. To test the DCs' cellular-level activity, mice were initially immunized with Man-CTS-TCL NPs and then challenged with B16 melanoma cells. The trials' findings indicated the high efficiency of Man-CTS-TCL NPs as anti-tumor vaccinations.

The mannosylated modification on HA improved targeting simultaneously. The connection between hyaluronic acid and CD44, a surface receptor known to influence dendritic cell development, increased the adhesion of NPs through the relationship of mannose with lectin. The invention of tailored nanocarriers utilizing a chitosan matrix is an effective technique for transferring genes that enhances immunomodulatory responses.

Vaccination utilizing dendritic cells is significantly constrained by the requirement for numerous injections and ex vivo surgical procedures. Han et al. (Han et al., 2016) developed a nanoparticle-based on chitosan for in vivo immunomodulation to treat cancer. Ovalbumin (OVA) was enclosed as an experimental antigen in a chitosan nanoparticle (CS NP) generated using ionic crosslinking with the incorporation of tripolyphosphate (TPP). The combined vaccination was shown to dramatically enhance anti-tumor effects by increasing DC maturation, as demonstrated in experimental settings (Seidi et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The movement of chitosan in cells
ce:section.ce:para: The process of endocytosis dictates the intracellular placement of NPs. Lysosomes, mitochondria, and Golgi bodies are among the subcellular organelles that receive NPs following their endocytosis and release from the vesicles in which they were carried. The NPs must, however, be able to discharge their payload once they have penetrated the cells. NPs' mobility across the cytoplasm and their eventual localization to target organelles upon cellular release are governed by their size, charge, and other physicochemical features (Chou et al., 2011).

Gene therapies rely on the effective transport of medications into the cytoplasm by NPs, where they may be absorbed by the nucleus for the greatest potential gene transmission. The potency of medications that are encapsulated may be greatly diminished by lysosomal enzymes. It is known that chitosan nanoparticles have increased endosomal escape capabilities due to the "proton sponge effect." At a pKa of 6.5, the amine units on chitosan are progressively protonated in the acidic environment of endosomes, requiring considerable quantities of water and chloride ions to balance the charges (Cao et al., 2019).

Chitosan enables the proton sponge effect, which opens the endosomes to water and chloride ions, increasing the endosomal size and may even cause its bursting (Lai & Wong, 2018). However, at times, this proton sponge effect may be less potent and may even depend on the cellular environment or pH conditions, and ultimately poses a threat to degrade the therapeutic cargo inside the lysosomes. Therefore, for improvement of performance, other polymers such as poly(methacrylic acid) or PLGA are also blended with chitosan (Zhang et al., 2018). These polymers enhance stability and buffering capacity for more efficient drug or gene delivery. Uptake and release can be further boosted by targeting ligands and pH-responsive peptides. Such strategies offer protection of sensitive therapies, such as mRNA, DNA, or anticancer drugs, against degradation and subsequent efficient delivery to their targets (Moreira et al., 2009).

Molecules that enter a cell by endocytosis move via early and late endosomes before arriving at the organelles that carry out the destruction, known as lysosomes. NPs enter the body via a multitude of pathways before arriving at the lysosomes, where their size and surface features affect the degree of absorption. Lysosomes, the most acidic organelles with a pH of 4.5 to 5.5, are where NPs are broken down enzymatically. NPs are produced to particularly target lysosomes to heal disorders associated with lysosomes or to halt lysosomes from breaking down on their journey to their goal inside the cell (Chang et al., 2010; Huang et al., 2020). Acetylation of chitosan leads to lysosomal rupture and an inflammatory response, which is identical to what happens with endosomes via the proton sponge effect, according to studies by Fong et al. (Rathore et al., 2019). When the U937 cell lines were transformed into macrophages, they noticed that the quantity of chitosan that produced lysosome damage varied depending on the dosage (Rathore et al., 2019).

Another study proved that chitosan-DNA polyplexes have a better transferable success rate in HEK cells as the positive charge of chitosan makes it easier for the cell to break the lysosome membrane. Pre-treating cells with free chitosan results in the accumulated chitosan in the lysosomes of cells so the uptake of DNA proves to be significant and more effective in enhancing the transfection efficiency of the DNA polyplexes, which earlier had lower effectiveness (Rathore et al., 2019). However, hyaluronic acid-conjugated chitosan nanoparticles did not enter the lysosomes of corneal and conjunctival cell lines and were proven to avoid lysosomal uptake completely; it may, therefore reveal a different interaction in cells and allow better delivery in those specific cell types (Fong et al., 2017).

The method by which chitosan NPs achieve this purpose is presently unknown, even though they have been utilized to deliver medicines to the mitochondria. Mitochondria-targeted delivery has been utilized to treat numerous malignancies. NPs may leverage the differential between ROS production and mitochondrial membrane potential to trigger cell death by generating ROS production, DNA release, and mitochondrial membrane rupture. A relatively minor change in membrane potential may lead to a substantial rise in ROS because of the exponential connection between ROS formation and the mitochondrial membrane potential (Thibault et al., 2011). Based on mitochondrial dysfunction and ROS generation, chitosan nanoparticles are identified to be innovative drug carriers mainly for targeting mitochondria, notably in anticancer therapy (Contreras-Ruiz et al., 2011). The mitochondrial membrane potential of the malignant cells is intrinsically larger than normal. These cells preferentially accept the positively charged CS NPs. Once inside these areas, the NPs also rupture the membrane, which allows cytochrome c to leak out and initiate apoptosis (Yameen et al., 2014). These also undergo higher oxidative stress owing to elevated ROS levels or the usage of drugs that create ROS, which culminates in a positive feedback loop whereby minor fluctuations in ΔΨm stimulate exponential ROS production, eventually causing cell death. Although the specific process by which such NPs are targeted to mitochondria is currently unknown, chitosan is a versatile drug carrier as it is biocompatible, biodegradable, and capable of delivering both hydrophilic and hydrophobic medications (Tammam et al., 2016; Opanasopit et al., 2009; Lien et al., 2012).

Target specificity is a critical factor since nontargeted healthy mitochondria can cause toxicity. More advanced conjugations of CS NPs with MPPs are under consideration for improving targeting mechanisms. Balancing the ROS levels is also required to achieve maximum therapeutic benefit with minimal side effects. Thus, further research in CS NPs can lead to the formation of a very potent mitochondrial-targeted agent for cancer therapy (Lien et al., 2012; Barbieri et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2009; Zorova et al., 2018).

The exocytosis of chitosan from cells influences its therapeutic toxicity and biosafety despite its low-risk profile. NPs' physicochemical features, which include size, shape, surface modification, concentration, and incubation period, all impact how quickly they are exocytosed from cells (Qi et al., 2005; Jiang et al., 2019). The exocytosis of N-acetyl histidine chitosan NPs was demonstrated by Park et al. to be dependent on the amount of time the cells were pre-incubated before the medium containing the nanoparticles was withdrawn. NPs of N-acetyl histidine chitosan self-assembled and broke down in acidic endosomes after a brief incubation period. Six hours of treatment resulted in the exocytosis of NPs from HeLa and A549 cells (Chen et al., 2015). Once again, it was revealed that the exocytosis of NPs from mast cells was dependent on the period of incubation for chitosan-coated PLGA NPs, with plain PLGA NPs demonstrating greater exocytosis than chitosan PLGA NPs (Hou et al., 2017). With this tactic, the payload might be more likely to enter the cytosol. However, NPs have been found in cells for up to 10 days when coated with chitosan and are ultra-small, superparamagnetic iron oxide (Wang et al., 2017). MCF-7 cells exhibited a time-dependent buildup of docetaxel-loaded deoxycholic acid-modified carboxymethyl chitosan NPs, demonstrating accelerated absorption within 6 h of incubation and continued accumulation in cells after 24 h compared to free doxorubicin (Mallick et al., 2019). Thus, chitosan is thought to increase the amount of time these particles remain within cells.

Chitosan is typically innocuous and does not adversely effect various cell types, such as MDBK kidney cells, A549 and Calu-3 lung cells, and Colo 205 colorectal cells (He et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2018; Hao et al., 2014). Chitosan NPs are not cytotoxically influenced by alterations involving amino acids, such as lysine or glutamic acid, or by glutaraldehyde cross-linking, even at high concentrations of up to 250 µg/mL (Kim et al., 2015). An unexpected observation is that chitosan nanoparticles (NPs) demonstrate stronger selectivity for cancer cells than normal cells, although chitosan's cytotoxicity is modified by cell type, particle size, and dosage. The sensitivity of these NPs to COS-1 cancer cells is greater than that of normal epithelial cells such as A549 and Calu-3 (Han et al., 2016). A concentration of 10 µg/mL was the IC50 for the leukemia cells after 24 h. On normal kidney cells like HEK 293 at a concentration of 50 µg/mL, chitosan NPs did not display any cytotoxicity. Compared to normal cells, malignant cells have higher mitochondrial activity, which leads to bigger levels of reactive oxygen species. This generates selective toxicity as the NPs are more readily internalized by these cells (Seidi et al., 2018).

Targeting T cells is an immune cell type, which helps to counter cancer through the production of IFN-γ in anti-tumor therapy. NPs of chitosan elicit and polarize T cells that have a better-attacking capability against the tumors due to the rearrangement of the cytoskeleton. Chitosan, therefore, causes selective damage to cancer cells and not the healthy ones, making it a promising tool in cancer therapies (Fig. 8 ).

The application of chitosan along with its analogs has been widespread for quite some time. The increasing number of papers and patents related to these polymers, as well as the expanding market outlook, attest to their prospective appeal. The usage of chitosan in the market is widely established in some of these sectors, including agribusiness as well as the food sector. Inconsistency in the study findings and a lack of understanding of the concluding procedure behind the features of chitosan, however, limit the potential of chitosan. The number of chitosan-related papers published between 2011 and 2020 has increased steadily. The high volume of reviews published in 2020, perhaps as a result of the COVID-19 epidemic, which has disrupted routine laboratory work throughout the globe, is partly responsible for the decline noted in 2021. The ongoing efforts of the Chitin Science Scientific Association are conducting thorough studies on this kind of polymer, however, this leads us to believe that this expansion will persist in the years to come. The interest of the industrial and scientific sectors in this polymer has been piqued by its approval by several bodies.

Chitosan requirements are dependent on where the substance will be used. Because of this, biological and pharmaceutical applications need high-quality chitosans with negligible levels of heavy metals and endotoxins. In addition, manufacturing must be tightly regulated so that undesirable hydrolysis and chemical changes aren't introduced during polymer separation. Production of chitosan is therefore not a minor concern. Due to the high temperatures and significant amounts of acid and basic reagents required, chitosan synthesis is not yet considered sustainable. One of the goals we have for the next decade is the widespread adoption of biocatalyst-based biotechnological processes, which are inherently greener but are now only feasible on a lab scale.

The study looked at the enormous potential of chitosan as a biodegradable and biocompatible polymer in the targeted DDS for mitochondria, particularly in cancer therapy. This is assured and determined based on the features of the NPs based on chitosan, which may be chemically and physically changed to optimize both the drug transport and release functions. Its primary virtue is its cationic nature, which increases medication absorption and cellular targeting. So, it has the potential to serve as a promising candidate for various therapeutic applications, including gene therapy, cancer treatment, and vaccine delivery. However, a lot of work needs to be done on its solubility, stability, and toxicity for clinical usage. Future research going to enhance the capabilities of chitosan will ensure safe and efficient usage in biomedical fields.

Deepika Yadav: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Rishabha Malviya: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Waleed Y. Rizg: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Musarrat Husain Warsi: Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.
